content:
# Exodus Chapter 9 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~36r-37r (PDF pp. 69-70); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 49-50 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_009_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_009_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Plague of Livestock Disease -- Dever (vv. 1-7)

**9:1** And YHWH said to Moses, "Go in to Pharaoh and speak to him, 'Thus says YHWH, the God of the Hebrews: Send out my people, that they may serve me. [fn1]

**9:2** For if you refuse to send them out, and you still hold them fast, [fn2]

**9:3** behold, the hand of YHWH is upon your livestock which is in the field -- upon the horses, upon the donkeys, upon the camels, upon the cattle, and upon the flocks -- a very heavy pestilence. [fn3]

**9:4** And YHWH will distinguish between the livestock of Israel and the livestock of Egypt, and nothing shall die of all that belongs to the sons of Israel.'" [fn4]

**9:5** And YHWH set an appointed time, saying, "Tomorrow YHWH will do this thing in the land."

**9:6** And YHWH did this thing on the next day, and all the livestock of Egypt died; but of the livestock of the sons of Israel, not one died. [fn5]

**9:7** And Pharaoh sent and behold, not even one of the livestock of Israel had died. But the heart of Pharaoh was heavy [kaved], and he did not send out the people. [fn6]

---

## The Plague of Boils -- Shechin (vv. 8-12)

**9:8** And YHWH said to Moses and to Aaron, "Take for yourselves handfuls of soot of a kiln, and let Moses scatter it toward the heavens before the eyes of Pharaoh. [fn7]

**9:9** And it shall become fine dust over all the land of Egypt, and it shall become upon man and upon beast boils breaking out in blisters in all the land of Egypt."

**9:10** And they took the soot of the kiln and stood before Pharaoh, and Moses scattered it toward the heavens, and it became boils -- blisters breaking out upon man and upon beast.

**9:11** And the magicians were not able to stand before Moses because of the boils, for the boils were upon the magicians and upon all Egypt. [fn8]

> **9:12** **And YHWH strengthened [chazaq, PIEL] the heart of Pharaoh, and he did not listen to them, as YHWH had spoken to Moses.** [fn9]

---

## The Plague of Hail -- Barad (vv. 13-35)

**9:13** And YHWH said to Moses, "Rise early in the morning and station yourself before Pharaoh, and say to him, 'Thus says YHWH, the God of the Hebrews: Send out my people, that they may serve me.

**9:14** For at this time I am sending all my plagues unto your heart, and upon your servants, and upon your people, so that you may know that there is none like me in all the earth. [fn10]

**9:15** For now I could have sent out my hand and struck you and your people with the pestilence, and you would have been cut off from the earth.

**9:16** And yet, for this reason I have caused you to stand -- to show you my power, and so that my name may be declared in all the earth. [fn11]

**9:17** You still exalt yourself against my people by not sending them out.

**9:18** Behold, about this time tomorrow I am causing to rain a very heavy [kaved] hail, the like of which has not been in Egypt from the day it was founded until now. [fn12]

**9:19** And now, send -- bring your livestock and all that you have in the field to shelter. Every man and beast that is found in the field and is not gathered into the house -- the hail shall come down upon them, and they shall die.'" [fn13]

**9:20** The one who feared the word of YHWH among the servants of Pharaoh caused his servants and his livestock to flee into the houses. [fn14]

**9:21** But the one who did not set his heart to the word of YHWH left his servants and his livestock in the field.

**9:22** And YHWH said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand toward the heavens, and there shall be hail in all the land of Egypt -- upon man, and upon beast, and upon every plant of the field in the land of Egypt."

**9:23** And Moses stretched out his staff toward the heavens, and YHWH gave thunders and hail, and fire went toward the earth; and YHWH rained hail upon the land of Egypt. [fn15]

**9:24** And there was hail, and fire flashing in the midst of the hail -- very heavy [kaved] -- the like of which had not been in all the land of Egypt since it became a nation. [fn16]

**9:25** And the hail struck in all the land of Egypt all that was in the field, from man to beast. And the hail struck every plant of the field and shattered every tree of the field.

**9:26** Only in the land of Goshen, where the sons of Israel were, there was no hail. [fn17]

**9:27** And Pharaoh sent and called for Moses and for Aaron and said to them, "I have sinned this time. YHWH is the righteous one, and I and my people are the wicked ones. [fn18]

**9:28** Entreat YHWH, and let there be enough of the thunders of God and the hail; and I will send you out, and you shall not continue to stand." [fn19]

**9:29** And Moses said to him, "When I go out of the city, I will spread out my palms to YHWH. The thunders shall cease and the hail shall be no more -- so that you may know that the earth belongs to YHWH.

**9:30** But as for you and your servants -- I know that you do not yet fear YHWH God." [fn20]

**9:31** And the flax and the barley were struck, for the barley was in the ear and the flax was in bud. [fn21]

**9:32** But the wheat and the spelt were not struck, for they are late crops.

**9:33** And Moses went out from Pharaoh, out of the city, and spread out his palms to YHWH. And the thunders and the hail ceased, and rain was not poured upon the earth.

**9:34** And Pharaoh saw that the rain and the hail and the thunders had ceased, and he continued to sin -- and he made heavy [kaved, HIPHIL] his heart, he and his servants. [fn22]

**9:35** And the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened [chazaq], and he did not send out the sons of Israel, as YHWH had spoken by the hand of Moses. [fn23]

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- "YHWH, the God of the Hebrews"** -- Hebrew: *YHWH Elohei ha-Ivrim* (יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הָעִבְרִים). This fuller divine title appears for the first time in the plague cycle, recurring in v. 13 and 10:3. "Hebrews" (*Ivrim*) is the outsider-designation, the name Egyptians would use for Israel. YHWH meets Pharaoh in Pharaoh's own political categories: the God of those slaves you hold. The demand formula is unchanged from 8:1: *shalach et-ammi veya'avduni* -- the piel imperative "send out" (not "let go"), followed by the purpose clause "that they may serve me." EMET: "Send out my people, and let them serve me"; LOGOS: "Send out My people, that they may serve Me"; PNEUMA: "Let my people go, so they may serve me." The consensus follows LOGOS's structure (per the established SOPHIA convention from ch. 8) with lowercase pronouns. LXX: *exaposteilon ton laon mou hina moi latreusōsin* ("send out my people that they may serve me").

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *makhaziq*: "hold fast"** -- Hebrew: *ve-odekha makhaziq bam* (וְעוֹדְךָ מַחֲזִיק בָּם). The participle *makhaziq* is the hiphil of *chazaq* -- the same root used for heart-hardening, here meaning "to hold fast, to grasp strongly." Pharaoh's "holding" of the people mirrors his heart's "strengthening." EMET: "hold them strongly"; LOGOS: "hold them"; PNEUMA: "hold them." The consensus renders "hold them fast" -- preserving the chazaq force (with "fast" as an intensifier) without the adverb "strongly" that overloads the sentence. The wordplay between *makhaziq* (holding the people) and the heart-hardening *chazaq* is noted but not forced into the English. LXX: *egkrateis autōn* ("hold power over them").

> **[fn3] v.3 -- "the hand of YHWH" and "heavy pestilence"** -- Hebrew: *yad-YHWH hoyah be-miqnekha* (יַד־יְהוָה הוֹיָה בְּמִקְנְךָ); *dever kaved me'od* (דֶּבֶר כָּבֵד מְאֹד). The "hand of YHWH" (*yad-YHWH*) is the first explicit mention of YHWH's hand as the instrument of plague. The escalation from "finger of God" (8:19, the magicians' confession) to "hand of YHWH" signals intensification. The word *dever* = "pestilence, plague" (nominal form from the root *d-b-r*, which also yields "word/thing" -- the wordplay between *dever* and *davar* is significant). The adjective *kaved* ("heavy") describes the plague's severity -- the same root used for heart-hardening, creating literary resonance: the plague is "heavy" just as Pharaoh's heart is "heavy." EMET: "a dever [pestilence] very heavy"; LOGOS: "a very heavy pestilence"; PNEUMA: "a devastating plague." The consensus follows LOGOS's word order ("a very heavy pestilence"), which renders the Hebrew *dever kaved me'od* naturally while preserving *kaved*. PNEUMA's "devastating" loses the *kaved* wordplay entirely. LXX: *thanatos megas sphodra* ("a very great death").

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *hiflah*: "distinguish"** -- Hebrew: *vehiflah YHWH bein miqneh Yisra'el uvein miqneh Mitsrayim* (וְהִפְלָה יְהוָה בֵּין מִקְנֵה יִשְׂרָאֵל). The hiphil of *palah* ("to distinguish, to set apart, to make wonderful") continues the Goshen distinction theme from 8:22. EMET: "distinguish"; LOGOS: "distinguish"; PNEUMA: "make a distinction." All three agree on the core sense. The consensus renders "distinguish" -- following EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's periphrastic "make a distinction" adds a word without adding meaning. The note about "sons of Israel" (*benei Yisra'el*): the consensus preserves this phrase per SOPHIA convention where the Hebrew reads *benei Yisra'el* (as in v. 4, 6, 26, 35). Where the Hebrew reads simply *Yisra'el* (as in v. 7), the consensus renders "Israel." LXX: *paradoxasei* ("will make a marvel/wonder").

> **[fn5] v.6 -- "all the livestock of Egypt died"** -- The Hebrew *kol* ("all") is likely hyperbolic or refers specifically to livestock "in the field" (v. 3), since livestock reappear in the hail narrative (vv. 19-21). All three translators note this. The consensus renders literally ("all") and lets the narrative context handle the scope, as the Hebrew text does.

> **[fn6] v.7 -- *kaved* qal: "was heavy" (stative)** -- Hebrew: *vayikhbad lev Par'oh* (וַיִּכְבַּד לֵב פַּרְעֹה). The qal of *kaved* -- "the heart of Pharaoh was heavy." This is a stative description, not an action: the heart simply *was* heavy, a settled condition. No agent is named. This contrasts with the hiphil forms in 8:15 and 8:32 where Pharaoh actively "made heavy" his own heart, and it contrasts with the chazaq qal ("was strengthened") used in 7:13, 7:22, 8:19. EMET: "was heavy"; LOGOS: "was heavy"; PNEUMA: "remained heavy." All three agree on the *kaved* identification. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "was heavy" -- the straightforward stative rendering. PNEUMA's "remained heavy" adds a durational nuance ("remained") not present in the Hebrew verb form. The devastating narrative context is that Pharaoh investigated, confirmed the distinction, and still refused. LXX: *ebarunthē hē kardia Pharaō* ("the heart of Pharaoh was made heavy").

> **[fn7] v.8 -- "soot of a kiln"** -- Hebrew: *piakh kivshan* (פִּיחַ כִּבְשָׁן). The *piakh* ("soot, fine ash") from a *kivshan* ("kiln, smelting furnace") evokes the furnaces/brick kilns in which Israelite slaves labored (cf. 1:14; 5:7-8). The instrument of oppression becomes the instrument of judgment. Moses scatters the soot heavenward (*hashamaimah*) in a public sign-act before Pharaoh's eyes. EMET: "full hands of piakh [soot] of a kiln"; LOGOS: "handfuls of soot from a kiln"; PNEUMA: "handfuls of soot from a kiln." The consensus renders "handfuls of soot of a kiln" -- following LOGOS/PNEUMA for "handfuls" (for *melo khofneikhem*, "full of your fists") but retaining "of a kiln" rather than "from a kiln" to more closely mirror the Hebrew genitive construction *piakh kivshan*.

> **[fn8] v.11 -- The magicians' final appearance** -- Hebrew: *velo yakhlu hakhartummim la'amod lifnei Mosheh* (וְלֹא־יָכְלוּ הַחַרְטֻמִּים לַעֲמֹד לִפְנֵי מֹשֶׁה). The magicians' complete trajectory across chapters 7-9: successful imitation (7:11, 7:22, 8:7), failure (8:18), confession of divine power (8:19, "the finger of God"), and now personal affliction. They cannot even *stand* (*la'amod*) before Moses -- the verb *amad* resonates with *he'emadtikha* ("I have caused you to stand") in v. 16: those whom Egypt's wisdom "caused to stand" in positions of authority cannot now stand at all. They disappear from the narrative permanently. All three translators agree on the rendering; no disagreement.

> **[fn9] v.12 -- THE TURNING POINT: YHWH strengthens Pharaoh's heart** -- Hebrew: *vayekhazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh* (וַיְחַזֵּק יְהוָה אֶת־לֵב פַּרְעֹה). **This is the most consequential hardening verse in the Torah to this point.** The verb *chazaq* appears in the PIEL stem (*vayekhazzeq*) -- intensive, active, causative -- with YHWH as the grammatical subject and Pharaoh's heart as the direct object. For the first time in the narrative sequence, YHWH is the direct agent of heart-hardening. Previously: Pharaoh's heart "was strengthened" (chazaq qal: 7:13, 7:22, 8:19), Pharaoh "made heavy" his own heart (kaved hiphil: 8:15, 8:32), or Pharaoh's heart "was heavy" (kaved qal: 9:7). Now YHWH acts directly. The piel stem implies not merely "allowing" rigidity but actively strengthening it. The narrative has carefully established that Pharaoh hardened himself first -- five plagues of self-inflicted resistance -- before YHWH ratified and intensified that trajectory. This fulfills the predictions of 4:21 (*chazaq* piel: "I will strengthen his heart") and 7:3 (*qashah* hiphil: "I will make hard his heart"). The phrase *ka'asher dibber YHWH el-Mosheh* ("as YHWH had spoken to Moses") ties prediction to fulfillment. EMET: "YHWH strengthened the heart of Par'oh"; LOGOS: "the LORD strengthened the heart of Pharaoh"; PNEUMA: "YHWH made Pharaoh's heart unyielding." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "YHWH strengthened the heart of Pharaoh" -- maintaining the SOPHIA convention of *chazaq* = "strengthen." PNEUMA's "made unyielding" introduces a different English verb that obscures the continuity with the *chazaq* qal occurrences ("was strengthened") elsewhere. The verse is rendered in bold and blockquoted to mark this critical transition. LXX: *esklērunen kurios tēn kardian Pharaō* ("the Lord hardened the heart of Pharaoh") -- using *sklērunō*, which is notably the same verb Paul invokes in Romans 9:18.

> **[fn10] v.14 -- "all my plagues unto your heart"** -- Hebrew: *kol-maggefotai el-libbekha* (כָּל־מַגֵּפֹתַי אֶל־לִבֶּךָ). The plagues are directed "unto your heart" (*el-libbekha*) -- to the very organ that Pharaoh has hardened. The targeting is deliberate: God sends plagues to the heart. EMET: "to your heart"; LOGOS: "unto your heart"; PNEUMA: "against your heart." The consensus renders "unto your heart" -- following LOGOS. The Hebrew preposition *el* means "to, unto, toward" -- directional, not adversarial. PNEUMA's "against" imports a hostile sense not present in the preposition. The noun *maggefah* = "plague, blow, striking" from *nagaf* ("to strike"). LXX: *eis tēn kardian sou* ("into your heart").

> **[fn11] v.16 -- *he'emadtikha*: "I have caused you to stand"** -- Hebrew: *ve'ulam ba'avur zo't he'emadtikha* (וְאוּלָם בַּעֲבוּר זֹאת הֶעֱמַדְתִּיךָ). The hiphil of *amad* ("to stand") = "I have caused you to stand, I have raised you up, I have sustained you in your position." This verse is quoted by Paul in Romans 9:17. The purpose is dual: (1) *ba'avur har'otkha et-kokhi* ("to show you my power"); (2) *ulema'an sapper shemi bekhol-ha-arets* ("so that my name may be declared in all the earth"). EMET: "I have caused you to stand"; LOGOS: "I have raised you up"; PNEUMA: "I have raised you up." The consensus renders "I have caused you to stand" -- following EMET. The reason: the Hebrew *he'emadtikha* is specifically the hiphil of *amad* ("to stand"), not *qum* ("to rise"). "Caused you to stand" preserves the precise Hebrew verb. "Raised you up" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) imports the sense of *qum* or of the LXX's *exegeirō* (Paul's citation in Romans 9:17) rather than the MT's *amad*. This is a significant text-critical point: the MT says God caused Pharaoh to *stand* (sustained him in his position); the LXX Vaticanus says God *preserved* him (*dietērēthēs*); Paul's citation says God *raised him up* (*exēgeira se*). The three-way divergence is one of the most discussed text-critical issues in Exodus. The SOPHIA convention follows the MT. The second phrase follows LOGOS: "to show you my power" (rather than EMET's "showing you my strength") because the Hebrew *kokhi* means "my power/strength" and "show" conveys the hiphil of *ra'ah* ("to cause to see, to show") clearly. LXX (Vaticanus): *heneken toutou dietērēthēs hina endeixōmai en soi tēn ischun mou* ("for this reason you have been preserved, that I may display in you my strength").

> **[fn12] v.18 -- *kaved* wordplay: "very heavy hail"** -- Hebrew: *barad kaved me'od* (בָּרָד כָּבֵד מְאֹד). The adjective *kaved* ("heavy") describes the hail -- the same root used for heart-hardening (vv. 7, 34) and for the pestilence (v. 3). The chapter is saturated with *kaved*: the pestilence is heavy (v. 3), the hail is heavy (vv. 18, 24), and Pharaoh's heart is heavy or made heavy (vv. 7, 34). The weight of divine judgment mirrors the weight of Pharaoh's stubbornness. All three translators preserve *kaved* for the hail. PNEUMA renders "the most devastating hail," which loses the *kaved* connection entirely. The consensus preserves "very heavy hail" to maintain the wordplay.

> **[fn13] v.19 -- The mercy warning** -- Hebrew: *shelakh ha'ez et-miqnekha* (שְׁלַח הָעֵז אֶת־מִקְנְךָ). The rare verb *ha'ez* (hiphil of *uz*, "to bring to shelter/safety") embedded in a plague announcement is an act of mercy -- YHWH warns and gives Pharaoh opportunity to protect lives. EMET: "shelter your livestock"; LOGOS: "bring into shelter your livestock"; PNEUMA: "bring your livestock... to safety." The consensus renders "bring your livestock... to shelter" -- combining LOGOS's "bring... to shelter" structure with the natural sentence flow. This warning creates a division within Egypt itself: those who fear YHWH's word vs. those who do not (vv. 20-21).

> **[fn14] v.20 -- "The one who feared the word of YHWH"** -- Hebrew: *hayare et-devar YHWH* (הַיָּרֵא אֶת־דְּבַר יְהוָה). Some Egyptians now fear YHWH's word -- the plagues are producing faith within Egypt. The verb *yare* ("to fear") anticipates v. 30, where Moses will accuse Pharaoh of not yet fearing YHWH. The contrast between "the one who feared" (v. 20) and "the one who did not set his heart" (v. 21) creates a within-Egypt distinction that transcends nationality and anticipates the "mixed multitude" (*erev rav*) of 12:38. EMET: "feared the word of YHWH"; LOGOS: "feared the word of the LORD"; PNEUMA: "took the word of YHWH seriously." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "feared the word of YHWH" -- preserving the verb *yare* ("to fear") rather than PNEUMA's paraphrase "took seriously." The theological concept of "fearing YHWH" is a cornerstone of Hebrew wisdom literature and should not be dissolved into a weaker expression.

> **[fn15] v.23 -- *qolot*: "thunders" (voices of God)** -- Hebrew: *qolot u-varad* (קֹלֹת וּבָרָד). The word *qolot* (plural of *qol*, "voice, sound") means both "thunders" and "voices." Thunder is the "voice" of YHWH -- a recurring motif (cf. Psalms 29). The fire "went toward the earth" (*vattihalakh esh artsah*) -- the verb *halakh* ("to walk, to go") gives the lightning a living, directional quality. This is theophanic language: the same elements (thunder, fire, hail) will appear at Sinai (19:16-18). EMET: "thunders"; LOGOS: "thunder"; PNEUMA: "thunder." The consensus renders "thunders" (plural) -- following EMET, because the Hebrew *qolot* is plural and the plural "thunders" preserves this form while remaining natural English (cf. "the thunders of Sinai"). LOGOS/PNEUMA's singular "thunder" normalizes the Hebrew plural.

> **[fn16] v.24 -- *esh mitlaqqakhat*: "fire flashing"** -- Hebrew: *ve'esh mitlaqqakhat betokh habbarad* (וְאֵשׁ מִתְלַקַּחַת בְּתוֹךְ הַבָּרָד). The hitpael of *laqakh* ("to take") = "taking hold of itself, flashing" -- fire that seizes and releases within the hailstones. Fire inside ice is a physical impossibility signaling cosmic disruption. EMET: "fire taking hold of itself in the midst of the hail"; LOGOS: "fire flashing in the midst of the hail"; PNEUMA: "fire flashing continuously within the hail." The consensus follows LOGOS: "fire flashing in the midst of the hail" -- "flashing" captures the iterative hitpael sense elegantly. EMET's "taking hold of itself" is maximally literal but cumbersome. PNEUMA's "continuously" adds a word not in the Hebrew. The phrase *kaved me'od* ("very heavy") applies to the hail, repeating the *kaved* motif.

> **[fn17] v.26 -- Goshen protected** -- The Goshen distinction, established in 8:22 (*hifleti*) and extended in 9:4 (*hiflah*), continues: the hail does not fall on Goshen. The adverb *raq* ("only") marks the exception. "Sons of Israel" is preserved per SOPHIA convention where the Hebrew reads *benei Yisra'el*.

> **[fn18] v.27 -- Pharaoh's confession: the *tsaddiq/rasha'* binary** -- Hebrew: *khatati happa'am YHWH hatsaddiq va'ani ve'ammi haresha'im* (חָטָאתִי הַפָּעַם יְהוָה הַצַּדִּיק וַאֲנִי וְעַמִּי הָרְשָׁעִים). Pharaoh's first judicial confession. Three elements: (1) *khatati* ("I have sinned") -- the standard confession formula; (2) *YHWH hatsaddiq* ("YHWH is the righteous one") -- the definite article marks *tsaddiq* as a title, not merely a description; (3) *va'ani ve'ammi haresha'im* ("and I and my people are the wicked ones") -- the antonym pair *tsaddiq/rasha'* is the fundamental binary of Israelite legal and wisdom literature. EMET: "YHWH [is] the righteous one, and I and my people [are] the wicked ones"; LOGOS: "The LORD is the righteous one, and I and my people are the wicked ones"; PNEUMA: "YHWH is the righteous one, and I and my people are the guilty ones." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "YHWH is the righteous one, and I and my people are the wicked ones." PNEUMA's "guilty ones" softens *resha'im* -- the word means "wicked," not merely "guilty." The *tsaddiq/rasha'* pair is a fixed antonym in Hebrew and should be rendered as such. Yet as Moses will observe in v. 30, this confession is produced by terror, not transformation. LXX: *ho kurios dikaios, egō de kai ho laos mou asebeis* ("the Lord is righteous, but I and my people are ungodly").

> **[fn19] v.28 -- *qolot Elohim*: "thunders of God"** -- Hebrew: *qolot Elohim* (קֹלֹת אֱלֹהִים). Literally "voices of God" -- the use of *Elohim* as a modifier may be superlative ("mighty thunders") or literal attribution ("God's thunder"). Given the theophanic context, the literal reading is preferred: these are God's own voices manifest in the storm. EMET: "thunders of God"; LOGOS: "thunder of God"; PNEUMA: "God's thunder." The consensus renders "thunders of God" -- preserving the plural *qolot* and the Hebrew genitive word order (noun + genitive modifier).

> **[fn20] v.30 -- "you do not yet fear YHWH God"** -- Hebrew: *terem tire'un mippenei YHWH Elohim* (טֶרֶם תִּירְאוּן מִפְּנֵי יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים). Moses' devastating rebuke. The word *terem* ("not yet") is significant -- it does not say "you will never fear" but "you do not *yet* fear." There is a faint opening toward the future. The full divine title *YHWH Elohim* is rare in Exodus and recalls the creation narrative (Genesis 2-3), reinforcing the creation-theology of the plague narrative. EMET: "you do not yet fear from before YHWH God"; LOGOS: "you do not yet fear before the LORD God"; PNEUMA: "you do not yet truly fear YHWH God." The consensus renders "you do not yet fear YHWH God" -- removing "from before" (EMET) and "before" (LOGOS), which render the Hebrew *mippenei* ("from the face of") too woodenly in this context. The idiomatic sense of *yare mippenei* is simply "to fear" or "to stand in awe of." PNEUMA's "truly" is an interpretation not in the Hebrew. The consensus conveys Moses' rebuke with directness.

> **[fn21] vv.31-32 -- Agricultural parenthesis** -- The barley was *aviv* ("in the ear") and the flax was *giv'ol* ("in bud") -- early-season crops destroyed by the hail. The wheat and spelt were *afilot* ("late/dark/hidden" -- still underground or barely emerging). This places the hail plague in approximately January-February in the Egyptian agricultural calendar. It also explains why crops survive for the locust plague in ch. 10. EMET: "the pishtah [flax] and the se'orah [barley] were struck"; LOGOS: "the flax and the barley were struck"; PNEUMA: "the flax and the barley were destroyed." The consensus follows LOGOS: "the flax and the barley were struck" -- preserving the Hebrew verb *nukkatah* ("were struck," niphal of *nakah*) rather than PNEUMA's "destroyed," which is too strong. The crops were struck, not necessarily annihilated. For "late crops" (v. 32): EMET: "afilot [late/dark/hidden]"; LOGOS: "late crops"; PNEUMA: "they ripen later." The consensus follows LOGOS: "late crops" -- a concise rendering of *afilot* that conveys the essential meaning.

> **[fn22] v.34 -- *kaved* hiphil: Pharaoh makes his own heart heavy again** -- Hebrew: *vayosef lakhato vayakhbed libbo hu va'avadav* (וַיֹּסֶף לַחֲטֹא וַיַּכְבֵּד לִבּוֹ הוּא וַעֲבָדָיו). The narrator's verdict: Pharaoh "continued to sin" (*vayosef lakhato*) -- the first time the narrator explicitly labels hardening as *sin* (*khet*). The verb *yasaf* ("to add, to continue") means he piled sin on sin. Then the specific sin: *vayakhbed libbo* ("he made heavy his heart") -- the hiphil of *kaved* with Pharaoh as active agent, consistent with 8:15 and 8:32. The addition of *hu va'avadav* ("he and his servants") extends culpability to the entire court. After his own confession that YHWH is righteous and he is wicked (v. 27), Pharaoh reverts the moment the pressure lifts. The gap between v. 27 and v. 34 exposes the mechanism of the hardening cycle: correct theology without genuine submission produces only deeper guilt. All three translators agree on the core rendering. The consensus preserves the SOPHIA convention: "he made heavy his heart" (Hebrew word order, *kaved* hiphil = "made heavy"). LXX: *ebarunen tēn kardian autou* ("he made heavy his heart").

> **[fn23] v.35 -- *chazaq* qal: "was strengthened" (closing formula)** -- Hebrew: *vayekhezaq lev Par'oh* (וַיֶּחֱזַק לֵב פַּרְעֹה). The qal of *chazaq* -- "the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened." This is the standard closing formula, returning to the pattern of the heart as grammatical subject with no named divine agent. After the piel divine hardening of v. 12, this qal form could be read as the ongoing effect of that divine action -- the door Pharaoh walked through is now locked behind him. The phrase *beyad Mosheh* ("by the hand of Moses") differs from the earlier *el-Mosheh* ("to Moses"): YHWH now speaks *through* Moses as an instrument, not merely *to* Moses as a recipient. EMET: "the heart of Par'oh was strengthened"; LOGOS: "the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened"; PNEUMA: "Pharaoh's heart became unyielding." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS: "the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened" -- maintaining the SOPHIA convention of *chazaq* qal = "was strengthened." PNEUMA's "became unyielding" breaks the *chazaq* convention. LXX: *esklērunthē hē kardia Pharaō* ("the heart of Pharaoh was hardened").

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### 1. "The hand of YHWH" and *dever kaved* (v. 3)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "behold, the hand of YHWH is upon your livestock... a dever [pestilence] very heavy [kaved]"
- **LOGOS:** "behold, the hand of the LORD is upon your livestock... a very heavy pestilence"
- **PNEUMA:** "then the hand of YHWH will strike your livestock... with a devastating plague"

**SOPHIA's decision:** "behold, the hand of YHWH is upon your livestock... a very heavy pestilence" -- following LOGOS's word order for the adjective phrase ("very heavy pestilence" rather than EMET's Hebrew-order "pestilence very heavy"), but preserving the *kaved* connection through the word "heavy." PNEUMA's "devastating" loses the *kaved* wordplay, which is one of the chapter's key literary devices. The verb "is upon" (rather than PNEUMA's "will strike") preserves the Hebrew participial force (*hoyah*, "being/becoming").

### 2. *He'emadtikha*: "I have caused you to stand" vs. "I have raised you up" (v. 16)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "I have caused you to stand"
- **LOGOS:** "I have raised you up"
- **PNEUMA:** "I have raised you up"

**SOPHIA's decision:** "I have caused you to stand" -- following EMET. This is the most significant translation decision in the chapter. The Hebrew verb is the hiphil of *amad* ("to stand"), not *qum* ("to rise") or *ur* ("to wake"). "Caused you to stand" preserves the precise Hebrew verb and distinguishes the MT from the LXX (which uses *diatēreō*, "to preserve") and from Paul's citation in Romans 9:17 (which uses *exegeirō*, "to raise up"). The three-way textual divergence is one of the most discussed issues in Exodus and should not be collapsed by importing the Pauline/LXX sense into the MT rendering. The SOPHIA convention follows the MT.

### 3. The *kaved* saturation of Exodus 9

The root *kaved* appears in six distinct roles across this chapter: (1) the pestilence is "very heavy" (v. 3); (2) Pharaoh's heart "was heavy" (v. 7); (3) the hail is "very heavy" (v. 18); (4) the hail is again "very heavy" (v. 24); (5) Pharaoh "made heavy" his heart (v. 34); (6) the adjective *kaved* modifies both plague-severity and heart-stubbornness. This is not coincidence but literary artistry: the weight of divine judgment mirrors and answers the weight of Pharaoh's resistance. The consensus preserves "heavy" for every *kaved* occurrence in this chapter to maintain the wordplay, refusing PNEUMA's dynamic alternatives ("devastating," "severe," "unyielding") that dissolve the connection.

### 4. Pharaoh's confession: *resha'im* -- "wicked ones" vs. "guilty ones" (v. 27)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "the wicked ones"
- **LOGOS:** "the wicked ones"
- **PNEUMA:** "the guilty ones"

**SOPHIA's decision:** "the wicked ones" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The *tsaddiq/rasha'* binary is the fundamental antonym pair in Hebrew legal and wisdom literature (cf. Proverbs, Psalms 1). *Rasha'* means "wicked" -- morally corrupt, fundamentally in the wrong -- not merely "guilty" (which could describe someone who made a procedural error). PNEUMA's softening diminishes the force of Pharaoh's confession.

### 5. Recurring convention disagreements

**a. Divine name:** LOGOS uses "the LORD"; EMET/PNEUMA use YHWH. SOPHIA follows YHWH per established convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**b. Pronoun capitalization for God:** LOGOS capitalizes divine pronouns (My, He, Me); EMET and PNEUMA do not. SOPHIA follows lowercase convention. (Recurring, LOW)

**c. "Sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites":** EMET: "sons of Yisra'el"; LOGOS: "sons of Israel"; PNEUMA: "Israelites." SOPHIA follows "sons of Israel" per convention. (Recurring, LOW)

---

## Hardening-Verb Tracking -- Exodus 9

| Verse | Hebrew Root | Form | SOPHIA Rendering | Agent | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 9:7 | kaved (כבד) | Qal *vayikhbad* | "the heart of Pharaoh was heavy" | Heart (stative) | After livestock plague; confirmed distinction, still refuses |
| **9:12** | **chazaq (חזק)** | **Piel *vayekhazzeq*** | **"YHWH strengthened the heart of Pharaoh"** | **YHWH (FIRST ACTIVE DIVINE HARDENING)** | **THE TURNING POINT: first time YHWH is the direct agent** |
| 9:34 | kaved (כבד) | Hiphil *vayakhbed* | "he made heavy his heart" | Pharaoh (self) | After hail; narrator labels it "sin"; includes servants |
| 9:35 | chazaq (חזק) | Qal *vayekhezaq* | "the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened" | Heart (stative) | Closing formula; "by the hand of Moses" |

### Cumulative Hardening-Verb Tracker -- Exodus 4-9

| # | Verse | Root | Form | SOPHIA Rendering | Agent |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4:21 | chazaq | Hiphil (prospective) | "strengthen" | YHWH (announced) |
| 2 | 7:3 | qashah | Hiphil (prospective) | "make hard" | YHWH (announced) |
| 3 | 7:13 | chazaq | Qal (stative) | "was strengthened" | Heart (self) |
| 4 | 7:14 | kaved | Adjective | "heavy" | Heart (state) |
| 5 | 7:22 | chazaq | Qal (stative) | "was strengthened" | Heart (self) |
| 6 | 8:15 | kaved | Hiphil (causative) | "made heavy" | Pharaoh (self) |
| 7 | 8:19 | chazaq | Qal (stative) | "was strengthened" | Heart (self) |
| 8 | 8:32 | kaved | Hiphil (causative) | "made heavy" | Pharaoh (self) |
| 9 | 9:7 | kaved | Qal (stative) | "was heavy" | Heart (state) |
| **10** | **9:12** | **chazaq** | **Piel (causative)** | **"strengthened"** | **YHWH (FIRST ACTIVE DIVINE HARDENING)** |
| 11 | 9:34 | kaved | Hiphil (causative) | "made heavy" | Pharaoh (self) |
| 12 | 9:35 | chazaq | Qal (stative) | "was strengthened" | Heart (self) |

**Pattern analysis -- THE TURNING POINT AT 9:12:**

Through Exodus 4-8, YHWH announced that he would harden Pharaoh's heart (4:21 chazaq hiphil; 7:3 qashah hiphil), but in the narrative sequence, every actual hardening was either Pharaoh's heart strengthening on its own (chazaq qal: 7:13, 7:22, 8:19) or Pharaoh actively making his own heart heavy (kaved hiphil: 8:15, 8:32). The progression was: announcement of divine hardening (4:21, 7:3) -- heart strengthens itself (7:13, 7:22) -- Pharaoh actively weighs down his heart (8:15, 8:32) -- heart is described as heavy (9:7).

At 9:12, for the first time, YHWH is the grammatical subject who actively strengthens (*chazaq* piel) Pharaoh's heart. The piel stem is intensive/causative -- YHWH does not merely observe the hardening but actively effects it. This fulfills the predictions of 4:21 and 7:3. What was announced is now enacted.

Remarkably, after this divine hardening, Pharaoh still hardens himself again in 9:34 (kaved hiphil), showing that divine and human agency interweave rather than divine action simply replacing human responsibility. The narrator even labels Pharaoh's self-hardening as "sin" (*khet*) for the first time (9:34). The theological complexity is immense: God's hardening confirms a direction Pharaoh himself chose; Pharaoh's subsequent self-hardening proves that human moral agency persists even after divine hardening. The two agencies converge in 9:34-35: Pharaoh makes his heart heavy (v. 34, kaved hiphil), and his heart is strengthened (v. 35, chazaq qal) -- double hardening, double agency.

The 12 instances across Exodus 4-9 (including the two prospective announcements) break into clear phases: (1) Divine announcement (4:21, 7:3); (2) Heart self-hardens (7:13, 7:22, 8:19 -- chazaq qal); (3) Pharaoh actively hardens (8:15, 8:32 -- kaved hiphil); (4) Heart is heavy (9:7 -- kaved qal); (5) **YHWH hardens** (9:12 -- chazaq piel); (6) Pharaoh hardens again + heart is strengthened (9:34-35). The trajectory is irreversible. Pharaoh's confession in v. 27 ("YHWH is righteous") makes the tragedy complete: he knows the truth and cannot act on it.

---

## Magicians' (Chartummim) Progression -- Complete Trajectory (Exodus 7-9)

| Reference | Action | Result | Narrative Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7:11-12 | Replicate staff-to-sea-serpent | Successful -- but Aaron's staff swallows theirs | Victory swallowed by defeat |
| 7:22 | Replicate water-to-blood | Successful -- but only add to the disaster | Amplifying the plague, not alleviating it |
| 8:7 | Replicate frogs | Successful -- but only add more frogs | Absurd: Egypt drowns in frogs; magicians produce more |
| 8:18 | Attempt to produce gnats | **FAILURE** -- "they were not able" | First complete failure; the limit of human art |
| 8:19 | Confess to Pharaoh | "This is the finger of God" | Acknowledge divine power; concede defeat |
| **9:11** | **Afflicted by boils** | **Cannot stand before Moses** | **Final humiliation: from practitioners to victims** |

*The magicians' trajectory is complete: successful imitation -- futile imitation -- failure -- confession -- personal affliction -- permanent disappearance. They represent Egypt's entire theological and intellectual establishment, systematically dismantled across six plagues. The irony of 9:11 is profound: those who once "stood" in positions of authority before Pharaoh cannot now "stand" before Moses. The verb amad ("to stand") links their humiliation to 9:16, where YHWH declares "I have caused you to stand" -- those whom Egypt caused to stand fall; only those whom YHWH causes to stand remain.*

---

*Consensus completed for Exodus 9:1-35 (35 verses). All readings follow the Leningrad Codex. LXX variants noted where significant. Three plagues rendered: livestock disease (vv. 1-7), boils (vv. 8-12), hail (vv. 13-35). The critical turning point at v. 12 is marked prominently: YHWH's first active hardening of Pharaoh's heart (chazaq piel). The kaved wordplay saturating the chapter is preserved across all six occurrences. "I have caused you to stand" (v. 16) follows the MT against the LXX and Paul's citation. Pharaoh's tsaddiq/rasha' confession (v. 27) and Moses' rebuke (v. 30) are rendered with full judicial force. The cumulative hardening-verb tracker now records 12 instances across Exodus 4-9 (including 2 prospective announcements and 10 narrative enactments).*

--- SOPHIA
